agent_output:
# Domain Analysis: Driver Earnings Transparency Feature

---

## Domain Analysis

### Business Context

This feature encodes the business process of **compensation visibility** — giving drivers real-time access to how their pay is calculated and what they receive. This is not merely a UI feature; it is a **regulated compensation disclosure system** that intersects labor law, tax classification, and worker rights across three fundamentally different legal regimes.

The core tension: a single feature must behave as three distinct compliance instruments depending on jurisdiction, while presenting a unified driver experience.

### Ubiquitous Language

| Term | Definition |
|------|-----------|
| **Base Pay** | The guaranteed minimum compensation for a work period. In employee contexts (CA), this is hourly/minimum wage. In contractor contexts (ID), this is a per-task rate agreed upon at engagement. |
| **Per-Delivery Bonus** | Variable compensation tied to completing individual delivery tasks. May include surge/demand multipliers. |
| **Tip** | Voluntary gratuity from a customer, passed through to the driver. Legally, tips belong to the driver — platform may never absorb or redistribute tips. |
| **Deduction** | Any amount subtracted from gross earnings. In employee contexts: taxes, benefits, legally mandated withholdings. In contractor contexts: platform fees, equipment rental, insurance opt-ins. **Every deduction must have an itemized reason.** |
| **Gross Earnings** | Base Pay + Per-Delivery Bonus + Tips, before any deductions. |
| **Net Earnings** | Gross Earnings minus all Deductions. The actual amount the driver receives. |
| **Earnings Period** | The time window over which earnings are aggregated for display. Must align with pay cycle (weekly, biweekly) for employees; may be per-task or daily for contractors. |
| **Driver Classification** | The legal employment status of a driver — **employee** or **independent contractor** — which fundamentally determines what data must, may, and must not be shown. |
| **Works Council Approval** | In Germany, formal consent from the Betriebsrat required before any system that affects compensation display or monitoring can be deployed to workers. This is a **hard legal gate**, not a courtesy. |
| **Wage Statement** | A legally defined document (e.g., California Labor Code §226) with mandatory fields. If this feature constitutes a wage statement, it must contain all legally required elements. |

### Bounded Context

**Driver Compensation & Compliance** — This sits at the intersection of the Payroll/Compensation bounded context and the Regulatory Compliance bounded context. It is NOT part of the Delivery Operations context, even though delivery data feeds into it.

---

## Business Rules

| # | Rule | Invariant? | Testable Assertion |
|---|------|------------|--------------------|
| **BR-1** | **Tips are sacrosanct.** The platform must never deduct from, redistribute, or offset tips against base pay or bonuses. Tips are always additive to other compensation. | ✅ Yes | `net_earnings >= (gross_earnings_excluding_tips - total_deductions) + tips` — tips must flow through untouched. |
| **BR-2** | **Every deduction must be itemized with a reason.** No "miscellaneous" or "other" deduction categories are permitted. Each deduction line must reference a specific, auditable cause. | ✅ Yes | For every `Deduction` record: `reason IS NOT NULL AND reason NOT IN ('other', 'miscellaneous', 'adjustment')` and `reason` maps to a known deduction taxonomy. |
| **BR-3** | **Earnings components must sum correctly.** `Gross = Base + Bonuses + Tips`. `Net = Gross - Deductions`. Rounding errors must not exceed ±$0.01 (or local currency equivalent). | ✅ Yes | `abs(gross - (base + bonuses + tips)) <= 0.01` AND `abs(net - (gross - deductions)) <= 0.01` in local currency. |
| **BR-4** | **California drivers MUST see all wage statement fields.** Under Labor Code §226, this includes: gross wages earned, total hours worked, piece-rate units (if applicable), all deductions, net wages earned, pay period dates, employer name/address. Missing any field is a **compliance violation**. | ✅ Yes | For `jurisdiction = 'CA'`: assert all §226 required fields are present and non-null in every earnings view. |
| **BR-5** | **Germany: Feature MUST NOT deploy without documented Works Council approval.** The system must enforce a jurisdiction-level feature gate that defaults to OFF for Germany and can only be enabled with an auditable approval record. | ✅ Yes | For `jurisdiction = 'DE'`: assert `works_council_approval.status = 'approved' AND works_council_approval.date IS NOT NULL` before feature flag is enabled. Deploying without this is a **legal violation**, not a bug. |
| **BR-6** | **Driver classification determines the earnings model displayed.** Employee earnings show wages, withholdings, employer contributions. Contractor earnings show task-based pay, platform fees, self-employment tax info. Mixing these models is legally dangerous. | ✅ Yes | `earnings_model_type` matches `driver.classification` for the given jurisdiction. An employee must NEVER see a contractor-style breakdown, and vice versa. |
| **BR-7** | **Currency and locale must match driver's operating jurisdiction.** Earnings displayed in wrong currency or format is not just a UX bug — it's a compliance failure for wage statements. | ✅ Yes | `earnings.currency = jurisdiction.local_currency` AND number/date formatting follows locale conventions. |
| **BR-8** | **"Real-time" has different legal meanings per jurisdiction.** In CA, earnings must be available by end of pay period. In ID, real-time per-task display is acceptable. Do not promise "real-time" in jurisdictions where the data pipeline cannot guarantee it — this creates legal exposure. | ❌ Soft | Define SLA per jurisdiction. CA: available within pay period. DE: per works council agreement. ID: best-effort real-time. |
| **BR-9** | **Historical earnings must be retained and accessible.** Drivers must be able to access past earnings breakdowns. California requires 3+ years of wage statement retention. | ✅ Yes | For `jurisdiction = 'CA'`: earnings records accessible for `>= 3 years`. Other jurisdictions: follow local retention law. |
| **BR-10** | **Deductions must never cause net pay to go negative.** If deductions exceed gross earnings in a period, the system must flag this for manual review rather than displaying a negative balance to the driver. | ✅ Yes | `net_earnings >= 0` for all displayed periods. If calculated net < 0, trigger review workflow and display "under review" status. |

---

## Business Impact

### Financial
- **California non-compliance risk is severe.** Labor Code §226 violations carry penalties of $50 per employee for the first violation and $100 per employee for subsequent violations, per pay period. At scale, this is material. Class-action exposure for systematic wage statement failures can reach millions.
- **Tip mishandling** creates both legal liability and reputational damage. California and many US states have strict tip-protection laws with treble damages.
- **ROI is asymmetric by market.** CA: compliance-driven (cost avoidance). DE: requires upfront investment in works council process. ID: competitive differentiator (voluntary transparency builds driver trust and retention).

### Operational
- **Three distinct compliance engines** must be maintained per jurisdiction. This is not a cosmetic localization task — it requires jurisdiction-aware business logic in the earnings calculation pipeline.
- **Works council process in Germany** can take 3-6 months. This is a hard dependency on the rollout timeline, not something engineering can accelerate.
- **Data pipeline reliability** becomes a compliance concern, not just an SLA concern. If earnings data is stale or incorrect in CA, it's a legal violation.

### User (Driver) Impact
- **Trust and retention.** Earnings transparency is the #1 driver-requested feature across gig platforms globally. Getting this right directly reduces churn.
- **Confusion risk.** Showing employee-model breakdowns to contractors (or vice versa) will generate support tickets and erode trust faster than showing nothing at all.
- **Accessibility.** Drivers in Indonesia may have lower financial literacy — the breakdown must be clear and jargon-free, not just legally compliant.

### Risk Profile
- **Highest risk: California.** Legal exposure is real, penalties are statutory, and class actions are common. This must be treated as a compliance project, not a product feature.
- **Medium risk: Germany.** Risk is binary — either you have works council approval or you don't deploy. No gray area.
- **Lowest legal risk: Indonesia.** But reputational risk exists — if the feature reveals unfavorable economics (high platform fees, low per-task rates), it could trigger driver unrest.

### Stakeholders Affected

| Stakeholder | Impact |
|-------------|--------|
| **Drivers (CA)** | Gain legally required wage visibility; may surface existing pay calculation errors |
| **Drivers (DE)** | Delayed access pending works council approval; partial rollout could create perception of inequity |
| **Drivers (ID)** | Gain voluntary transparency; may react negatively to visible deduction ratios |
| **Legal/Compliance Team** | Must validate per-jurisdiction compliance; ongoing audit burden |
| **Works Council (DE)** | Must be engaged as a partner, not informed as a stakeholder. They have veto power. |
| **Finance/Payroll** | Must ensure earnings calculation pipeline is audit-ready; reconciliation burden increases |
| **Customer Support** | Will see increased inquiries as drivers question previously invisible deductions |
| **Product/Engineering** | Must build jurisdiction-aware feature gating, not just localization |

---

## Acceptance Criteria

### Core Criteria

| # | Given | When | Then |
|---|-------|------|------|
| **AC-1** | A driver in California completes a delivery | They view their earnings breakdown | They see: gross wages, hours worked, base pay rate, per-delivery bonus amount, tips (separate line), each deduction itemized with reason, net pay, pay period dates, and employer identity — all §226-required fields |
| **AC-2** | A driver in Germany attempts to access earnings breakdown | The works council has NOT approved the feature | The feature is not visible; no earnings breakdown UI is rendered; the driver sees their existing pay view unchanged |
| **AC-3** | A driver in Germany attempts to access earnings breakdown | The works council HAS approved (with audit record) | The feature renders earnings per the approved specification |
| **AC-4** | A driver in Indonesia completes 5 deliveries in a day | They view their earnings breakdown | They see per-delivery base pay, bonuses, tips, and platform fees — using contractor terminology (not wage/salary language) |
| **AC-5** | A driver receives a $10 tip on a delivery | The earnings breakdown is displayed | The $10 appears as a separate "Tips" line item. It is NOT subtracted from base pay. It is NOT included in deduction calculations. `Net >= (Gross_excluding_tips - Deductions) + Tips` |
| **AC-6** | A deduction is applied to a driver's earnings | The earnings breakdown is displayed | The deduction appears with a specific, human-readable reason (e.g., "Health insurance premium" not "Deduction #4") |
| **AC-7** | An engineer attempts to enable the DE feature flag | No works council approval record exists in the system | The system rejects the flag change and logs the attempt |

### Edge Cases

| Scenario | Expected Behavior |
|----------|-------------------|
| **Driver relocates from Indonesia to California** | Classification changes from contractor to employee. Earnings model must switch completely. Historical earnings remain in original model; new earnings use CA employee model. No hybrid view. |
| **Tip amount is $0.00** | Display "Tips: $0.00" — do not hide the line. Transparency means showing zero, not omitting. |
| **Deductions exceed gross earnings** | Do NOT display negative net pay. Flag for manual review. Show driver a "Your earnings for this period are under review" message. |
| **System outage delays earnings data** | In CA, display a clear "Data as of [timestamp]" disclaimer. Do not display stale data without a freshness indicator. Stale wage statements are a compliance risk. |
| **Works council approval is revoked mid-rollout in Germany** | Feature must be immediately disabled. This is not a gradual deprecation — it's an emergency kill switch. |
| **Driver disputes an earnings calculation** | System must retain the exact calculation inputs and formula used at the time of display, not just the result. Audit trail is a legal requirement in CA. |
| **Multi-delivery batch with mixed tip/no-tip orders** | Each delivery's tip must be attributed individually. Averaging tips across deliveries obscures the actual tip flow and may violate tip-protection laws. |
| **Currency conversion for cross-border deliveries** | Earnings must be displayed in the driver's jurisdiction currency. If a delivery crosses borders, the conversion rate and method must be documented, not hidden. |

---

## Confidence Assessment

| Dimension | Level |
|-----------|-------|
| **Overall Confidence** | **82%** |
| **High Confidence Areas** | California §226 wage statement requirements; tip protection invariants; works council approval as a hard gate; driver classification determines earnings model; arithmetic integrity rules |
| **Low Confidence Areas** | Specific Indonesian labor regulations (tax reporting, platform fee disclosure requirements); exact German works council negotiation scope (may require more than just display approval — data retention, access logging); whether "real-time" creates implied contractual obligations in any jurisdiction |
| **Assumptions** | (1) Platform already has accurate driver classification data per jurisdiction. (2) Payroll/earnings calculation engine exists and is correct — this feature is about *display*, not *calculation*. (3) Works council engagement has not yet begun in Germany. (4) No other jurisdictions are in scope for initial rollout. (5) Tips are collected and tracked per-delivery, not aggregated at payout. |

---

## Rejection Reasons (Preemptive)

Any implementation that does the following **must be rejected**:

| Violation | Business Rule Broken |
|-----------|---------------------|
| Ships a single, jurisdiction-agnostic earnings view | BR-4, BR-6 — Conflates employee and contractor models; misses CA-required fields |
| Rolls out to Germany without works council approval gate | BR-5 — Legal violation, not a bug |
| Nets tips against base pay or includes tips in deduction base | BR-1 — Tip protection invariant violated |
| Shows "Deductions: -$X.XX" without itemization | BR-2 — Every deduction must have an auditable reason |
| Displays earnings in USD for Indonesian drivers | BR-7 — Currency must match operating jurisdiction |
| Allows negative net pay display to drivers | BR-10 — Must trigger review workflow, not display negative balance |
| Treats works council approval as a soft launch criterion ("we'll get it later") | BR-5 — Approval is a legal prerequisite, not a follow-up item |

---

**Bottom line:** This is not one feature — it is three jurisdiction-specific compliance instruments sharing a UI shell. The temptation to build "one flexible earnings view" and configure it per market will lead to domain drift and compliance gaps. Each jurisdiction's rules must be encoded as first-class business logic, not configuration flags.
